skill.name: webapp-testing
skill.description: Toolkit for testing local web applications with Playwright, including browser automation, UI inspection, screenshots, and console log capture.
skill.summary: Test and inspect local web applications with Playwright automation.
skill.author: davila7

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported many high-risk patterns, but most are false positives from license text, markdown examples, and local URLs. The confirmed risk is the helper script running user-provided server commands with shell=True, which is expected for local web testing but requires caution.

medium_findings.title: User-Provided Server Commands Run Through Shell
medium_findings.description: The helper script accepts --server values and starts them with subprocess.Popen using shell=True. This supports cd and && for local dev servers, but unsafe or attacker-influenced command strings could execute arbitrary shell commands.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The code directly passes a command-line argument to subprocess.Popen with shell=True. The behavior is intentional for the skill, but it is still a real command execution risk if inputs are untrusted.

medium_findings.title: Skill Encourages Direct Execution Of Bundled Helper Script
medium_findings.description: The skill documentation instructs agents to invoke scripts/with_server.py as a black-box helper for local testing. This is legitimate workflow guidance, but marketplace users should know it can start local processes and run follow-up commands.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The documentation clearly tells users to run the helper with server commands. The usage is transparent and local, so this is a moderate operational risk rather than evidence of malicious intent.

low_findings.title: Markdown Backticks Are Documentation, Not Shell Execution
low_findings.description: The SKILL.md external command alerts are caused by Markdown inline code and fenced examples. They document Playwright and helper-script usage rather than executing Ruby backticks or hidden shell commands.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Manual inspection shows these lines are Markdown instructions, code examples, and selector names. There is no Ruby code path or runtime backtick execution in SKILL.md.

low_findings.title: Localhost And License URLs Are Benign
low_findings.description: Hardcoded URLs point to localhost test targets or Apache License references. No evidence found that the skill contacts external services or exfiltrates data.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The URLs are visible, ordinary localhost or Apache license references. They do not include secrets, encoded payloads, or suspicious remote endpoints.

low_findings.title: Filesystem Writes Are Local Test Artifacts
low_findings.description: The examples write screenshots and console logs to local output paths. These writes are expected for browser testing and do not show persistence, tampering, or hidden collection.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The file paths are used for visible screenshots and logs generated during tests. No evidence found of sensitive file reads, stealthy writes, or persistence mechanisms.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography Alerts Are False Positives
low_findings.description: The reported weak cryptography locations are ordinary words in the Apache License, argparse metadata, and markdown prose. No cryptographic function or hashing code is present at those locations.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Manual inspection shows these lines contain license language, parser setup, or selector guidance. There is no MD5, SHA1, DES, or other weak cryptographic algorithm in use.

dangerous_patterns.title: subprocess.Popen With shell=True
dangerous_patterns.description: The helper starts server commands through the shell to support common development command syntax. This pattern is dangerous with untrusted input and should be used only for commands the user intentionally provides.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The dangerous pattern is explicit in code and semantically relevant. The risk is bounded by the documented purpose of launching local development servers.

dangerous_patterns.title: subprocess.run Executes Follow-Up Command
dangerous_patterns.description: After servers are ready, the helper runs the remaining command arguments. This is expected for test automation, but it can execute arbitrary local commands if misused.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The command execution is direct and intentional. It is less risky than shell=True because args.command is passed as an argument list, but it still runs local commands.

content.user_title: Test Local Web Apps With Playwright
content.value_statement: Frontend bugs are hard to diagnose from static code alone. This skill guides Claude, Codex, and Claude Code through Playwright-based browser testing for local web apps.
actual_capabilities: Guides Playwright scripts for local dynamic web applications.
Starts one or more local development servers before running automation.
Captures browser screenshots for visual inspection.
Collects console logs from browser sessions.
Discovers buttons, links, inputs, and rendered DOM selectors.
Supports static HTML testing through file URLs.
limitations: Requires Playwright and browser dependencies in the execution environment.
Targets local applications and examples, not hosted production monitoring.
Server commands run locally and must be trusted by the user.
Generated tests may need selector updates for complex or changing UIs.

use_cases.title: Verify A New UI Flow
use_cases.description: Run a local app, open the target page, interact with controls, and confirm visible results.

use_cases.title: Debug Browser Console Errors
use_cases.description: Capture console messages while reproducing a page interaction in a headless browser.

use_cases.title: Inspect Page Structure Before Automation
use_cases.description: Discover rendered buttons, links, and form fields before writing a focused test script.

prompt_templates.title: Check A Local Page
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner verification of a running local app.
prompt_templates.prompt: Open my local web app with Playwright, wait for the page to load, and report visible errors or broken UI states.

prompt_templates.title: Capture A Screenshot
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the webapp-testing skill to launch the local page, take a full-page screenshot, and summarize visible layout issues.

prompt_templates.title: Find Selectors For A Flow
prompt_templates.prompt: Inspect the rendered DOM for this local app and identify stable selectors for the main buttons, links, and inputs.

prompt_templates.title: Run A Multi-Server Test
prompt_templates.scenario: Advanced testing with frontend and backend services.
prompt_templates.prompt: Start my backend and frontend servers, run a Playwright interaction test, capture console logs, and explain any failures.

output_examples.input: Test the dashboard page on my local dev server.
output_examples.output: The browser opened the dashboard, waited for network idle, and captured a screenshot. The main navigation rendered, but the activity panel stayed empty after refresh.

output_examples.input: Find selectors for the sign-in form.
output: Email input uses a stable id selector.
Password input is visible after the page loads.
Submit can be targeted by role and button name.

output_examples.input: Capture console output while clicking Save.
output_examples.output: The click triggered one warning and one failed request message. The warning appears unrelated, but the failed request matches the missing save confirmation.

best_practices: Run helper scripts with --help before using them.
Use trusted local server commands only.
Wait for network idle before inspecting dynamic pages.
anti_patterns: Do not run server commands copied from untrusted pages or issues.
Do not rely on brittle selectors when stable roles or ids exist.
Do not inspect dynamic pages before client-side rendering finishes.

faq.question: Does this skill require Playwright?
faq.answer: Yes. It is designed around Python Playwright scripts and browser automation.

faq.question: Can it test static HTML files?
faq.answer: Yes. The examples include testing local HTML files through file URLs.

faq.question: Can it start my development server?
faq.answer: Yes. The helper script can start one or more local servers before running a test command.

faq.question: Is it safe to run any server command?
faq.answer: No. Only run commands you trust, because server commands execute on your local machine.

faq.question: What output can it produce?
faq.answer: It can produce observations, screenshots, selector lists, console log summaries, and test failure explanations.

faq.question: Does it monitor production websites?
faq.answer: No. It focuses on local testing workflows and example automation patterns.
